item.title: A simple Emacs helm example
item.dc:creator: Daniel Nicolai
item.description:
Although I recommend Ivy over Helm for most people, because Ivy's use and documentation is much more approachable, there might be some time that you can not avoid using Helm. I find Helm's documentation quite cryptic, with very few examples. Therefore here follow two simple examples to help you out quickly. 

(defun helm-print ()
  (interactive)
    (helm :sources (helm-build-sync-source "Name"
		     :candidates '(a b c)
		     :action '(("print" . (lambda (x)
					     (print x)))))))

item.title: Numerical Aperture (NA) approximations
item.dc:creator: Daniel Nicolai
item.description:
A not so short inquiry into NA approximations and their validity.

The definition for NA is given by the formula \[\mathrm{NA} = \sin{\theta}\] where \(\theta\) is half the opening angle of the light cone accepted by the imaging system.

For small angles the paraxial approximations \[\sin{\theta} \approx \theta,\: \cos{\theta} \approx 1\: \text{and}\: \tan{\theta} = \frac{\sin{\theta}}{\cos{\theta}} \approx \theta\] can be used for calculating \(\theta\), \(\sin{\theta}\) and \(\tan{\theta}\).

Lees verder… (1 min resterende leestijd )

item.title: Spacemacs Robot framework layer
item.dc:creator: Daniel Nicolai
item.description:
This week support for the Robot Framework Intellisense language server was added to Emacs's lsp-mode.

For easy installation a spacemacs layer that also uses the robot-mode package by Doug MacEachern can be found here.
